result.text:
Norms for the six-agent consortium

1. Capacity transparency  
   Every agent continuously publishes a compact, truthful “load vector” (current queue length, expected completion time for each item, and a short “fatigue” score reflecting how costly the next task would be). No agent may understate its load to attract easier work or overstate it to avoid hard work.

2. Proportional intake  
   Incoming requests are first triaged by urgency and expertise match. They are then offered to the agent whose published load vector indicates the lowest marginal cost, provided that agent possesses the required expertise. If two or more agents are equally eligible, the request is assigned to the one whose cumulative share of work over the last 100 requests is smallest, enforcing long-run balance.

3. Burden premium  
   A request whose estimated effort exceeds a jointly agreed threshold (e.g., 3× the median routine task) carries an explicit “burden token.” The agent that accepts it may later redeem that token to pass one comparable future burden to whichever peer currently has spare capacity. Tokens are non-transferable and expire after a fixed horizon so they cannot be hoarded.

4. Refusal and re-offer  
   An agent may refuse only when its published load already exceeds a safety threshold or when it genuinely lacks the skill. Upon refusal it must immediately re-publish an updated load vector and the request is offered to the next-best agent. Chronic refusal without updated load justification triggers a brief, automatic review by the other five.

5. Mutual cover for overload  
   If an agent’s load spikes above the safety threshold, the remaining agents collectively guarantee to absorb at least one task each from its queue before accepting new external work. This obligation rotates; no single agent is the permanent “first responder.”

6. Skill development and rotation  
   Agents that repeatedly handle a narrow class of expert tasks must, at regular intervals, document how they perform them so that at least one other agent can acquire the same capability. This prevents single points of failure and gradually equalizes expertise.

7. Periodic norm review  
   Every 500 requests the agents hold a short, synchronous audit: they compare actual versus predicted loads, count burden-token redemptions, and adjust the burden threshold or token lifetime if systematic imbalances appear. Any change requires the consent of at least four agents.

These seven norms are mutually supporting: transparency enables fair assignment, the burden token and cover rules protect against chronic overload, and the review mechanism keeps the arrangement legitimate to every participant over time.
